stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 woman has just delivered a 4,100 g live male infant by spontaneous vaginal birth at 40 weeks and 4 days gestation. Her three previous births were uncomplicated. The third stage was actively managed with oxytocin 10 IU IM. The placenta delivered intact at 8 minutes with no obvious cotyledon defect. Within 5 minutes of placental delivery she has lost an estimated 1,200 mL of fresh red blood with ongoing trickling. On examination the uterus is soft and poorly contracted. There is no obvious genital tract trauma on inspection. She has no known coagulopathy, no anticoagulants, no prior PPH, and was Hb 116 g/L on admission. IV access is in place with 2 large-bore cannulae, fluids are running, group and hold is sent and the obstetric emergency team has been activated. Bimanual compression has been commenced.
lead_in: Following first-line IV oxytocin bolus and infusion, which is the next-line uterotonic to administer per Australian PPH guidelines?

options.text: IM/IV ergometrine 250 mcg, repeatable up to 5 doses over 2 hours as uterotonic
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in this hypotensive tachycardic patient because of its vasoconstrictor effects and risk of further haemodynamic compromise; an alternative uterotonic is preferred per RANZCOG PPH.

options.text: IM carboprost (15-methyl PGF2alpha) 250 mcg, repeatable up to 8 doses
options.rationale: Australian PPH algorithms (NSW Health, KEMH, RANZCOG) escalate as: oxytocin (bolus + infusion) -> ergometrine (if not hypertensive) -> carboprost 250 mcg IM every 15 minutes (max 2 mg) -> misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR. In this hypotensive, tachycardic, multiparous patient where ergometrine is relatively contraindicated by haemodynamic instability and uncertain BP trajectory, carboprost is the preferred next-line uterotonic, with tranexamic acid 1 g IV in parallel (RANZCOG PPH; NSW Health PPH).

options.text: Misoprostol 200 mcg PO single dose, no further uterotonic added at this stage
options.rationale: Misoprostol may be used as adjunctive uterotonic but the recommended dose is 800-1000 mcg PR/SL, not 200 mcg PO, and it is later in the escalation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV only, with no further uterotonic added at this stage
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is recommended early in PPH (WOMAN trial; given within 3 hours), but should be given in addition to ongoing uterotonics, not instead of them (RANZCOG).

options.text: Recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg IV bolus without further uterotonic at this stage
options.rationale: Recombinant FVIIa is a last-resort haemostatic agent in massive PPH refractory to standard measures; it is not a uterotonic and is not appropriate at this stage (RANZCOG).

stem: A 38-year-old G3P3 woman attends her general practice for contraceptive counselling. Her youngest child is 4 months old and exclusively breastfed. Her BMI is 33. She has migraine with aura (typical scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral headache, average twice per month) since age 19 (this hooks the COCP and patch distractors, since migraine with aura is an absolute UKMEC 4 contraindication to combined hormonal contraception). She had a left ankle ORIF 6 weeks ago for a complex fracture and remains in a removable boot, mobilising with crutches; her physiotherapist plans to discontinue the boot in 2 weeks. Her mother had a provoked DVT on the combined oral contraceptive at age 25. She is planning a long-haul flight (16 hours) in two weeks, which compounds the VTE risk already imposed by her reduced mobility post-fracture. She is normotensive (118/74) and a non-smoker. She had a Caesarean for breech with her first child and two subsequent uneventful vaginal births. Her cervical screening test is up to date and negative. She has no liver disease, no breast cancer history, no current pelvic infection and no abnormal uterine bleeding. She is open to any contraceptive method that is safe in her current circumstances and prefers a long-acting reversible option if appropriate.
lead_in: Which contraceptive method is most appropriate at this consultation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: COCP is UKMEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for migraine with aura at any age, and additionally UKMEC 3-4 with recent immobilisation post-orthopaedic surgery, BMI >=35 (here 33, but elevated baseline VTE), family history of VTE, age >=35 with multiple risk factors, and breastfeeding before 6 weeks postpartum (now 4 months so less restrictive but cumulative risk is high) (FSRH UKMEC 2016 + 2024 updates).

options.text: Combined transdermal contraceptive patch - weekly application regimen
options.rationale: Same systemic VTE/arterial risks as COCP; UKMEC 4 for migraine with aura (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Levonorgestrel 52 mg intrauterine system (Mirena) - clinic insertion
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1-2 in all of her stated risk categories (migraine with aura UKMEC 2, postpartum >=4 weeks UKMEC 1, VTE risk UKMEC 2, BMI 33 UKMEC 1) and provides highly effective long-term contraception suitable for breastfeeding and immobilisation; this is the most appropriate choice (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) - IM every 12 weeks
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC 2 for migraine with aura and for breastfeeding but is UKMEC 3 in women with multiple VTE risk factors (recent surgery, immobilisation); LARC options with no VTE signal (IUDs, implant) are preferable (FSRH UKMEC).

options.text: Progestogen-only pill (desogestrel) - daily continuous regimen
options.rationale: POP is safe (UKMEC 1-2) in her circumstances but is less effective (typical-use failure approximately 9%) than LNG-IUS and depends heavily on adherence; in a woman with this risk profile a LARC is preferable (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman in established labour at 40 weeks and 2 days gestation is being monitored by continuous CTG in the birth suite. Her previous birth was a normal vaginal delivery. The pregnancy was uncomplicated. Her GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 22 hours ago with clear liquor; she has been in established labour for 9 hours. Cervical examination 30 minutes ago: 8 cm, fully effaced, station 0, position OA, intact caput. She is on no antibiotics. Over the past 90 minutes the midwife has documented a rising maternal temperature (now 38.2 degC), maternal tachycardia (HR 118), uterine tenderness between contractions, and new offensive vaginal discharge. The liquor draining now is meconium-stained (thick, particulate). Her epidural is functioning well. There are no antenatal risk factors for placental insufficiency. She is hydrated and her last paracetamol was 6 hours ago. The neonatal team and obstetric registrar have been called for review of the CTG.
lead_in: Using RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance (IFM) classification, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue to observe progress, repeat VE in 2 hours, reassess CTG closely
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological (RANZCOG IFM: baseline tachycardia, reduced variability, recurrent late decelerations, plus clinical chorioamnionitis and meconium); continued observation alone is unsafe (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin augmentation to expedite spontaneous vaginal delivery within the hour
options.rationale: Tachysystole and uterine hyperstimulation worsen foetal compromise; augmentation is contraindicated in the presence of pathological CTG and likely chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Insert foetal scalp electrode and consider foetal blood sampling for lactate assessment
options.rationale: FBS may have a role in pathological CTG with intact membranes and no other red flags, but in the presence of clinical chorioamnionitis, meconium, prolonged ROM, and recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability, the appropriate action is delivery, not further sampling (RANZCOG IFM).

options.text: Stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, proceed directly to Category 1 Caesarean now
options.rationale: Proceeding immediately to Category 1 CS at 8 cm in a multipara without trialling intrauterine resuscitation, treating the underlying chorioamnionitis, and obtaining senior review for the most appropriate mode of birth is premature; expedited birth is required but the route depends on station, position, response to resuscitation, and senior assessment per RANZCOG IFM.

options.text: Intrauterine resuscitation, IV antibiotics for chorioamnionitis, paracetamol for pyrexia, senior review for expedited birth
options.rationale: The combined features (pathological CTG, clinical chorioamnionitis, thick meconium, prolonged ROM 22 hours, maternal tachycardia and pyrexia) require simultaneous intrauterine resuscitation, treatment of the underlying chorioamnionitis with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, antipyretics, and senior decision-making for expedited birth (vaginal vs Cat 1 CS depending on response and progress); this is the RANZCOG IFM-aligned response (RANZCOG IFM; RANZCOG chorioamnionitis).

stem: An 18-month-old girl (weight 11 kg) with no prior seizure history is brought to a regional ED in active generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began 28 minutes ago at home. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 5 minutes and a second buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg at 15 minutes without termination. On arrival at 25 minutes she remains in generalised convulsion with eye deviation, frothing, and incontinence; IV access has just been secured on the second attempt and a bedside glucose is 4.8 mmol/L. She had a brief febrile illness with rhinorrhoea over the preceding 48 hours and a temperature of 38.6 deg C recorded today, but there is no rash, no vomiting, and no known toxic ingestion accessible in the home. She has met all developmental milestones to date, is fully immunised to the current Australian schedule, and has no family history of epilepsy or sudden infant death. The mother denies head trauma and no household members are on antiepileptic or antidepressant medications. There has been no prior antiepileptic exposure. The team has applied high-flow oxygen at 8 L/min via Hudson mask, has charcoal-impregnated suction available at the bedside, has prepared a rapid-sequence induction trolley, and the anaesthetic registrar and PICU outreach team have been paged.
lead_in: Following the APLS status epilepticus algorithm, what is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with continuous ECG monitoring
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable second-line agent in older children, but the EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials and the updated APLS Australia algorithm prefer levetiracetam, particularly in children under 2 years where phenytoin carries higher arrhythmia and infusion-site risk, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with airway support ready
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is the preferred second-line agent only in neonates; in infants and children outside the neonatal period it is third-line after levetiracetam (and after phenytoin if used), as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

options.text: A third dose of buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg and continue to observe for spontaneous resolution
options.rationale: More than two doses of benzodiazepine increases respiratory depression without improving termination rates; the algorithm advances to a second-line antiepileptic at this point, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg (max 3 g) over 5 minutes with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: After two benzodiazepine doses, the current APLS Australia and RCH algorithm recommends IV levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg as the preferred second-line agent in infants and children, supported by EcLiPSE/ConSEPT/ESETT data, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

options.text: Rapid sequence induction with thiopentone 4 mg/kg and intubation as primary seizure control
options.rationale: Thiopentone induction is reserved for refractory status epilepticus after second-line agents have failed; jumping to RSI before trialling a second-line antiepileptic skips the algorithm, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

stem: A 9-year-old boy with severe persistent asthma (one PICU admission 18 months ago for life-threatening wheeze, baseline fluticasone-salmeterol BD via spacer, salbutamol PRN) is brought to a tertiary ED after 12 hours of progressive wheeze triggered by a viral URTI in the family. In ED over the past 60 minutes he has received continuous back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg every 20 minutes for three doses, oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg with antiemetic cover, and high-flow oxygen at 10 L/min via nebuliser mask. There has been no clinical improvement: he remains exhausted, leaning forward, speaking only in single words, and looks visibly tired between breaths. He has not yet received any IV bronchodilators, IV magnesium, or aminophylline. He is normotensive but markedly tachycardic with a fine tremor and palpitations from the salbutamol. There has been no fever, no foreign body history, no chest pain, and no preceding anaphylactic features. The PICU outreach team has reviewed at the bedside and recommends escalation but has not yet started any infusions while awaiting the ED decision. He has a 22-gauge cannula in the right antecubital fossa and a second cannula being placed; his last meal was 4 hours ago. There are no known drug allergies and parents have given verbal consent for further therapy.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes and reassess in 1 hour
options.rationale: He has already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting without escalation risks decompensation and is not the next step, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Severe acute asthma failing oxygen, back-to-back salbutamol/ipratropium and systemic steroid escalates to IV magnesium sulphate before IV salbutamol or aminophylline, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute and Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory.

options.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg loading over 60 minutes followed by 1 mg/kg/h infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is an option but is reserved for cases failing IV magnesium and IV salbutamol because of its narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: IV salbutamol 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by 1 microg/kg/min infusion
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is reasonable but typically follows IV magnesium in the RCH escalation ladder; potassium is already 3.0 mmol/L and lactate 3.8 mmol/L which IV salbutamol will worsen, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

options.text: Immediate rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 2 mg/kg and rocuronium 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma carries high risk of bronchospasm, hypotension, and barotrauma and is reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

stem: An 11-year-old girl (weight 38 kg) is admitted to PICU with new-onset diabetic ketoacidosis (initial venous pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, corrected sodium 141 mmol/L at admission). She received an initial 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride for shock and was then commenced on a deficit-plus-maintenance regimen calculated for 7% dehydration over 48 hours, with an insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/h started 1 hour after the fluids and added potassium 40 mmol/L once she had urinated. She had no preceding head injury, no toxic ingestion, and no prior diabetes diagnosis; her family history is notable only for type 2 diabetes in a paternal uncle. Six hours into therapy the bedside nurse notes a 15-minute history of increasing headache, restlessness, and one episode of vomiting; over the past 20 minutes she has become more drowsy and confused, no longer engaging with her parents. Pre-decline GCS was 15; current GCS is 12 (E3 V3 M6). The most recent blood glucose is 14.2 mmol/L (was 32, falling at 3 mmol/L/h), sodium has fallen from 138 to 132 mmol/L despite the glucose correction (corrected sodium therefore falling), and she now has bradycardia and a rising blood pressure on the trend chart. No focal neurology has yet been demonstrated; pupils are equal at 3 mm and reactive. The on-call PICU consultant has been called and the bed is being prepared for hyperosmolar therapy and possible intubation.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion to 0.025 units/kg/h and add 5% dextrose to current fluid bag, continue current rate
options.rationale: Adding dextrose and reducing insulin is appropriate when glucose falls below ~14-17 mmol/L but does not address suspected cerebral oedema with bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS, which requires immediate hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Increase IV fluid rate to replace deficit over 24 hours instead of 48 hours to improve cerebral perfusion
options.rationale: Increasing fluid rate worsens cerebral oedema; current evidence (PECARN FLUID trial) supports cautious rehydration and immediate hyperosmolar therapy when oedema is suspected, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: IV hypertonic 3% sodium chloride 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, reduce fluid rate, urgent CT
options.rationale: Bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS, headache and vomiting are the Cushing-like triad of DKA-related cerebral oedema; immediate hyperosmolar therapy with 3% saline 2.5-5 mL/kg (or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg) plus fluid rate reduction and head CT once stable are first-line, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for cerebral oedema and continue current fluid and insulin regimens
options.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces vasogenic oedema (e.g. tumour, meningitis) but has no role in DKA-related cytotoxic/osmotic cerebral oedema, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

options.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-28 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure today
options.rationale: Aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation below pCO2 30 mmHg in DKA cerebral oedema is associated with worse outcomes; intubation may be needed but is not the first step before hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

stem: A 13-year-old girl (weight 52 kg) is brought to ED by her parents after they found an empty 100-tablet bottle of paracetamol 500 mg in her room and a written note. She admits to taking tablets in two separate episodes over the past 18 hours: she states she took about 30 tablets approximately 14 hours ago after an argument with her sister, then a further 20 tablets about 4 hours ago when she decided she wanted to die. She denies co-ingestion of other tablets, alcohol, or recreational drugs and there are no other empty packets in the home. She has no chronic medical illness, has a normal body mass index, is not on enzyme-inducing drugs (no carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampicin, or isoniazid), and there is no chronic alcohol use, eating disorder, or malnutrition that would raise glutathione-depletion risk. She is fully alert with no nausea or right upper quadrant pain. The on-call mental health team has been notified and is on the way. A paracetamol level taken on arrival (now 4 hours after the second ingestion and 18 hours after the first) is 320 micromol/L. ALT is 38 U/L and INR is 1.1; creatinine is 64 micromol/L; bicarbonate, lactate, and glucose are all normal. She has not vomited and has not yet received activated charcoal or N-acetylcysteine. The on-call clinical toxicologist has been contacted by phone.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management decision regarding N-acetylcysteine?

options.text: Plot the 320 micromol/L level on the nomogram at 4 hours, withhold N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions with a known time; in staggered ingestion or unknown time it cannot be used, and a low nomogram level does not exclude toxicity, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness and RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning.

options.text: Withhold N-acetylcysteine because ALT and INR are normal, and arrange repeat bloods in 8 hours to reassess
options.rationale: ALT and INR rise late (often after 24-36 hours); waiting for derangement in a staggered overdose of >200 mg/kg risks missing the treatment window where NAC is most effective, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours immediately
options.rationale: Staggered paracetamol ingestion >200 mg/kg (or >10 g in adults) over <24 hours mandates immediate full-course IV N-acetylcysteine regardless of the nomogram level; the nomogram does not apply, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness, RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning, and the Australian/New Zealand consensus statement on paracetamol overdose.

options.text: Give activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally and reassess paracetamol level in 4 hours before deciding on N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion; here the earlier ingestion is 18 hours old and the more recent one is 4 hours old, so charcoal has minimal benefit, and N-acetylcysteine should not be delayed, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

options.text: Commence oral methionine 2.5 g every 4 hours for 4 doses as an alternative to IV N-acetylcysteine
options.rationale: Oral methionine is not used in current Australian practice for paracetamol overdose; IV N-acetylcysteine is the standard antidote, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

stem: A 6-day-old male term infant (birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.2 kg) is brought to a regional ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy, and grey colour over the past 12 hours. He was born at 39+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, with Apgars 9 and 9, and discharged from the maternity unit at 36 hours after a normal newborn examination. The newborn pulse oximetry screen at the birth hospital was reportedly normal at 24 hours. There is no family history of congenital heart disease. On arrival in ED he is grey-mottled, with cool peripheries and weak femoral pulses bilaterally; the four-limb blood pressures show a right arm of 78/50 mmHg and a right leg of 52/30 mmHg. Pre-ductal SpO2 (right hand) is 92%, post-ductal SpO2 (foot) is 78%. He has a hyperdynamic precordium and a single loud second heart sound with no audible murmur. The chest x-ray shows cardiomegaly with pulmonary plethora. Bedside echo by the visiting paediatric cardiology fellow on tele-link demonstrates a critical coarctation of the aorta with a closing ductus arteriosus. The retrieval service is en route.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg IV to close the patent ductus arteriosus and improve systemic perfusion
options.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent systemic circulation (critical coarctation, interrupted aortic arch, hypoplastic left heart) will precipitate cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct open, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.

options.text: Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia and improve cardiac output
options.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia at >220 bpm with absent P waves; the picture is duct-dependent systemic shock, not SVT, and adenosine has no benefit, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

options.text: Sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg orally to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance and improve systemic oxygenation
options.rationale: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; it does not maintain ductal patency, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.

options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion starting at 10-50 nanog/kg/min (commonly 0.05 microg/kg/min), with airway and apnoea management readiness
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation with a closing PDA is reopened with prostaglandin E1 infusion; the starting dose is 0.01-0.05 microg/kg/min titrated to effect, with airway support readiness because of apnoea risk, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.

options.text: Commence high-dose oxygen at FiO2 1.0 via head-box to maximise systemic saturations and reduce hypoxia-driven acidosis
options.rationale: High FiO2 in duct-dependent systemic circulation can promote ductal closure (oxygen is a stimulus for closure) and worsen the situation; target pre-ductal SpO2 ~85-92% rather than 100%, as per RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease.

stem: A male infant born at 28+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia and absent end-diastolic flow on Doppler is now day 7 of life in the level 3 NICU of a tertiary perinatal centre. Mother received a full course of antenatal betamethasone and magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection. Birthweight was 1040 g (25th centile for gestation). He has been receiving minimal enteral feeds of expressed breast milk at 20 mL/kg/day for 4 days, advanced cautiously, and parenteral nutrition via a peripherally inserted central catheter; umbilical lines were removed on day 4. He is on nasal CPAP with FiO2 0.21 after extubation from surfactant therapy on day 2. Over the last 6 hours the nurses note new abdominal distension, two episodes of bilious aspirate, a single small bloody stool and a temperature instability with a low of 36.1 deg C. On examination he is mottled with a capillary refill of 4 seconds, the abdomen is tense and tender with visible bowel loops, and there is mild erythema of the abdominal wall. He has had one apnoea requiring stimulation in the last hour. He has no umbilical lines in situ. He has had no surgical procedures and no recent transfusions. He has not yet received probiotics. The treating team obtains a supine abdominal radiograph and a left lateral decubitus film, plus blood cultures, a venous gas and a full blood count.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue current feeds, add oral probiotic, and repeat AXR in 24 hours
options.rationale: Continuing enteral feeds in confirmed NEC (Bell stage IIA with pneumatosis) is contraindicated and risks progression to perforation, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

options.text: Increase enteral feed volume to advance gut maturation and start oral gentamicin for bowel decontamination
options.rationale: Advancing feeds is contraindicated and oral aminoglycosides are not indicated for established NEC; bowel rest with parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics is standard, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Urgent laparotomy with bowel resection for suspected NEC-related intestinal perforation today
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is indicated for pneumoperitoneum, fixed bowel loop, palpable mass or clinical deterioration despite medical therapy; this baby has Bell IIA disease without perforation and warrants medical treatment, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

options.text: Cease feeds, large-bore OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, fluid resuscitation, surgical review
options.rationale: Bell stage IIA NEC (definite, with pneumatosis intestinalis) is managed medically with bowel rest, orogastric decompression, parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negative and anaerobic organisms (benzylpenicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole) and supportive care, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

options.text: Cease feeds, give a single dose of IV cefotaxime and continue monitoring without surgical review
options.rationale: Monotherapy with cefotaxime does not adequately cover anaerobes, and surgical review is mandatory at diagnosis to allow timely intervention if deterioration occurs, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

stem: A term female infant is born at 38+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth with a birthweight of 2360 g (below the 3rd centile), a head circumference of 31.5 cm (below the 3rd centile), and scattered petechiae on the trunk and limbs noted on first examination. APGARs were 8 and 9 and no resuscitation was required. The mother is a 24-year-old preschool teacher who had a flu-like illness with malaise, sore throat and mild posterior cervical lymphadenopathy at around 14 weeks gestation; the GP attributed it to a viral URTI and routine maternal serology was not repeated and TORCH screening was not done. There is no family history of hearing loss or congenital infection, no maternal HIV or syphilis, and rubella immunity was documented at booking. On day 2 of life, the baby has a moderately enlarged liver palpable 3 cm below the costal margin, a spleen tip palpable, mild conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and thrombocytopenia on FBC. A cranial ultrasound performed by the neonatologist shows scattered periventricular calcifications and mild ventricular dilatation, prompting paediatric infectious diseases review. Initial automated otoacoustic emission screen on day 2 is REFER bilaterally and is repeated next day with the same result. Audiology brainstem testing is pending. The treating team obtains urine and saliva PCR samples within the first week of life to confirm the suspected diagnosis.
lead_in: Once congenital CMV is confirmed by PCR within 21 days of life, what is the single most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg twice daily for 6 months with weekly FBC/LFT and audiology
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV with end-organ involvement (microcephaly, hepatitis, thrombocytopenia, intracranial calcifications, or sensorineural hearing loss) treated with oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg BD for 6 months improves hearing and neurodevelopmental outcomes; haematology and LFT monitoring is required because of neutropenia and hepatotoxicity, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV and AAP Red Book.

options.text: Observe with serial audiology, no antiviral, treat only confirmed progressive hearing loss after 12 months
options.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV warrants antiviral therapy promptly; delaying until 12 months misses the window when valganciclovir improves outcomes, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.

options.text: Commence IV ganciclovir 6 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks followed by no further therapy
options.rationale: Six weeks of IV ganciclovir was the older regimen and has been superseded by 6 months of oral valganciclovir which is non-inferior with better neurodevelopmental and audiological outcomes, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

options.text: CMV hyperimmune globulin infusion and arrange paediatric infectious diseases service review today
options.rationale: CMV hyperimmune globulin is not standard postnatal therapy for symptomatic congenital CMV; oral valganciclovir is the recommended treatment, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

options.text: Treat only audiological component with hearing aid fitting after ABR; provide no antiviral therapy
options.rationale: Hearing rehabilitation is part of care but does not replace antiviral therapy, which itself improves hearing trajectories in symptomatic infants, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.
